plan: Identify why the best-fit dt reaching the maximum allowed physical boundary (1960 days) violates the assumptions underlying Wilks' Theorem, and formally state which method must be used instead.

Explain the specific conditions that make Wilks' Theorem inapplicable here (boundary effects, non-Gaussian likelihood surface, local vs. global minima structure) and contrast with the guarantees provided by the Feldman-Cousins method.

Define the 2D parameter space (ns, dt) over which the Feldman-Cousins confidence region will be constructed, and specify the grid of "truth" parameter combinations (ns_true, dt_true) that must be sampled — including how finely to grid the space and how many signal trial sets to run per combination.

Describe how to obtain TS_best-fit for each pseudo-trial: run the full four-parameter likelihood minimizer (free fit over γ, ns, t0, dt) on each scrambled+injected dataset to find the global minimum, yielding a best-fit TS for that trial.

Describe how to obtain TS_truth for each pseudo-trial: perform a likelihood scan (not a new free fit) at the fixed injected truth point (ns_true, dt_true) while profiling over the remaining nuisance parameters (γ, t0), yielding the likelihood value at the truth.

Define ΔTS for pseudo-trials as TS_best-fit − TS_truth (per the context's convention), and construct the ΔTS distribution across all trials at each (ns_true, dt_true) grid point; identify the ΔTS threshold that captures 90% of the distribution.

Describe how to obtain ΔTS for the true (unscrambled) data: compute TS_best-fit from the minimizer on the real dataset, then scan the likelihood at each (ns, dt) grid point on the real data to get TS_truth at each point, and form ΔTS_data = TS_best-fit − TS_truth(ns, dt).

Construct the 2D Feldman-Cousins confidence region: at each grid point (ns, dt), compare ΔTS_data to the 90th-percentile threshold derived from the pseudo-trial ΔTS distribution at that point; include the point in the contour if ΔTS_data ≤ threshold.

Analyze the sign of pseudo-trial ΔTS as a function of (ns_true, dt_true): identify when the minimizer reliably finds a better minimum than the truth point (positive ΔTS) and when boundary conditions or a poor landscape force the minimizer to a worse or equal solution (near-zero or negative ΔTS).

Explain how IceCube's iMinuit-based minimizer seeds initial parameter values using the signal-over-background (S/B) ratio of events — writing the explicit seeding equation — describe the S/B threshold used to select candidate seed events for t0 and dt initialization, explain why local minima in the (ns, dt) surface cause the minimizer to return non-global solutions that make ΔTS deviate from zero, and propose one concrete improvement (e.g., multi-start seeding across a grid of (t0, dt) initial values) to increase the probability of finding the true global minimum.
